description: Probabilistic identifiers (filenames, versions, hashes) for FIPS 140-3 validated software cryptographic modules.

tracks.C: on-disk .so/.dll hashes extracted from distro packages
tracks.A: deterministic extraction from CMVP certs + Security-Policy text
tracks.B: web-fished published artifact hashes, each with source URL + skeptic verification

confidence_field: identity_confidence in [0,1]; identity_evidence lists the contributing signals

tested_configurations: VMware Photon OS 2.0 with JDK 11 on VMware ESXi 6.7 running on Dell PowerEdge R830 with Intel Xeon E5 (single-user mode) VMware Photon OS 2.0 with JDK 7 on VMware ESXi 6.7 running on Dell PowerEdge R830 with Intel Xeon E5 VMware Photon OS 2.0 with JDK 8 on VMware ESXi 6.7 running on Dell PowerEdge R830 with Intel Xeon E5

published_artifacts.evidence: Cert 4600 'HYCU Cryptographic Library Module' lists filenames: bcfips-1.0.2.3.jar and software_versions 1.0.2.1/1.0.2.2/1.0.2.3, matching the Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API (bc-fips) artifact on Maven Central, version 1.0.2.3. This module appears to be HYCU's private-labeled/embedded use of the Bouncy Castle FIPS Java jar (Legion of the Bouncy Castle Inc. holds its own separate FIPS certs, e.g. #3514; HYCU's cert wraps the same bc-fips jar per its security policy's filename listing). The sha256 was read directly from Maven Central's published .sha256 checksum file for the exact matching version 1.0.2.3.

provenance.trackB_notes: The module's security policy lists the shipped filename as bcfips-1.0.2.3.jar (also covering versions 1.0.2.1 and 1.0.2.2) matching exactly the 'org.bouncycastle:bc-fips' artifact on Maven Central. I downloaded/verified only version 1.0.2.3's published sha256 checksum from repo1.maven.org (Maven Central mirror); 1.0.2.1 and 1.0.2.2 jars are also on Maven Central but I did not fetch their hashes since the module's own filename list names only the .3 build. Confidence is moderate (0.6) rather than higher because I could not independently confirm from the security policy PDF itself that this Maven jar is bit-for-bit identical to the CMVP-tested binary (did not fetch the PDF); this is standard practice for vendors embedding the pre-existing Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API as their crypto module.

provenance.trackB_notes: Eclypses Cryptographic Library (ECL) is a closed, proprietary vendor SDK component embedded in Eclypses' MTE (Micro Token Exchange) technology. Files libecl.so/libecl.dylib/ecl.dll are shipped only as part of the commercial MTE/ECDN SDK distributed to licensed customers via Eclypses' developer portal (public-docs.eclypses.com) or direct sales contact — no public download URL, package repo, or checksum listing was found. Eclypses' GitHub org (github.com/eclypses) hosts wrapper/sample code, not the compiled library binaries. No NVD/CPE entry or public checksum located. Consistent with typical closed vendor appliance/SDK modules — returning found=false per instructions rather than padding with speculative artifacts.

provenance.trackB_notes: Kenwood Cryptographic Library (libkcl.so) is a proprietary closed-source crypto library from EF Johnson Technologies (a JVCKENWOOD company), embedded in radio/appliance firmware (Zebra TC21 Android device, STM32MP151 Linux appliance). No public download of libkcl.so as a standalone artifact was found: no GitHub release, no Maven/package-manager entry, no distro package, no vendor download page offering this specific binary. EF Johnson's open-source portal (info.efjohnson.com/open-source/) only provides GPL/LGPL-licensed third-party components on request, not this FIPS-validated module itself. Security policy PDF (140sp4699.pdf) is public but contains no build/binary distribution info or hashes. Concluding found=false per instructions for closed vendor appliances.

provenance.trackB_notes: Module is "Cryptographic Module for BIG-IP®" v1.0.2u-fips, F5's internal OpenSSL 1.0.2u-fips-derived libcrypto.so.1.0.2, embedded inside BIG-IP 16.1.3.1 software/VE images. BIG-IP images are distributed only to licensed customers via F5's downloads portal (downloads.f5.com) behind support-contract authentication — no publicly downloadable standalone artifact or published SHA-256 exists for this exact build. Security policy confirms module identity: https://csrc.nist.gov/CSRC/media/projects/cryptographic-module-validation-program/documents/security-policies/140sp4716.pdf . Returning found=false per instructions for closed vendor appliance software with no public specimen.

published_artifacts.version: v5.2.1 (wolfCrypt FIPS module within wolfssl-5.6.3 commercial package)
published_artifacts.evidence: Security Policy (140sp4718.pdf, Table 4 'Tested Module Identification – Software, Firmware, Hybrid') lists Package/File Name = wolfssl-5.6.3-commercial-fips-linuxv5.2.1.7z, Software/Firmware Version v5.2.1, Integrity Test = HMAC-SHA256. This is wolfSSL's commercial FIPS-certified build, distributed to licensed customers via wolfSSL directly (not a public anonymous download); no public sha256 checksum could be located for this exact archive. wolfSSL's general open-source repo (github.com/wolfSSL/wolfssl) hosts the non-FIPS-boundary source under the same version family but is not itself the validated FIPS artifact.

provenance.trackB_notes: Cert 4718 = wolfCrypt FIPS 140-3 module, Overall Level 1, Software Multi-Chip Stand Alone, validated 2024-07-11. Security Policy PDF (Document Version 1.6, 5 Dec 2025) Table 4 identifies the tested executable code set as file 'wolfssl-5.6.3-commercial-fips-linuxv5.2.1.7z', Module Software Version v5.2.1, with HMAC-SHA256 integrity check performed at runtime (not a published release-file hash). wolfSSL's FIPS-validated builds are licensed/commercial deliverables obtained directly from wolfSSL (per their FIPS licensing page https://www.wolfssl.com/license/fips/), not posted as a public download with a discoverable checksum — so no sha256 could be read from any published source; sha256 is null per the hard rule against fabrication. The public wolfSSL/wolfssl GitHub repo distributes the general-purpose library (currently up through 5.9.x) but does not carry this exact FIPS-boundary commercial archive, so it was not listed as a separate artifact to avoid implying it's the validated binary.

rows.module_name: Tensor G2 UFS Inline Storage Encryption Cryptographic Module
tested_configurations: Linux Kernel 5.10 running on Google Pixel 7 with Google Tensor G2 With PAA

provenance.trackB_notes: Module is "pixel-fips140.ko" for Google Pixel 7 / Tensor G2, Linux Kernel 5.10 (cert 4720, UFS Inline Storage Encryption). This is Google's GKI (Generic Kernel Image) FIPS 140-3 kernel crypto module, built from AOSP kernel/common source (crypto/fips140-module.c, crypto/fips140-selftests.c) combined with device-specific Tensor G2 kernel sources (kernel/gs, kernel/gs101 or kernel/zuma trees on android.googlesource.com). Google documents the build process at https://source.android.com/docs/core/architecture/kernel/gki-fips140-module but does NOT publish a prebuilt pixel-fips140.ko binary for download, nor any published SHA-256 checksum for it — the module is meant to be built from source by OEMs/researchers using the documented build commands, with an HMAC-SHA256 self-integrity digest embedded at build time (that digest is generated per-build, not a fixed published hash). Checked: AOSP kernel/common Git repo (source browsable, not a packaged release artifact), source.android.com GKI FIPS docs, Pixel security support page — none host a downloadable signed .ko or checksum manifest. No Maven/distro/vendor download exists since this ships embedded in Pixel factory images/OTA (which are large composite images, not a discrete validated-module artifact with a citable published hash for this specific .ko file). Returning found=false per instructions rather than padding with a low-confidence guess at an OTA/factory-image URL that would not actually isolate this module.

provenance.trackB_notes: This module is a SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 15 SP4 distro build of libgcrypt (validated artifact libgcrypt.so.20.3.4 / libgcrypt.so.20), likely from libgcrypt 1.9.4 upstream sources rebuilt by SUSE. SLE update-channel RPMs are gated behind a SUSE Customer Center subscription (registercloudguest/SCC), not freely mirrored. Public mirrors (rpmfind.net, openSUSE Build Service SUSE:SLE-15-SP4:Update, software.opensuse.org) either do not carry SLE (as opposed to openSUSE Leap/Tumbleweed) binaries or returned 404 for the specific repository/package path. No published SHA-256 for the exact validated binary was located within the ~6 query search budget. Recommend a follow-up with authenticated SCC access or SUSE's public FIPS security-policy PDF (which sometimes lists build hashes) if a deeper pass is warranted. Returning found=false per instructions rather than padding with unverified guesses.

KeyPair FIPS Provider for OpenSSL 3 (cert #4724) is not publicly downloadable as a standalone artifact. Per keypair.us (https://keypair.us/2024/07/keypair-fips-provider-for-openssl-3/), KeyPair Consulting distributes this module directly to technology vendors who then get it rebranded onto their own FIPS 140-3 certificates ("the #1 most rebranded FIPS 140-3 certificate"); access requires contacting info@keypair.us. The tarball KeyPair ships bundles their patches with OpenSSL 3.0.10 source, but no public URL, checksum, or release page was found for this tarball or for compiled fips.so/fips.dll binaries. NIST CSRC only hosts the security policy PDF (140sp4724.pdf), not the module binary. No SHA-256 was read from any source, so none is reported.

published_artifacts.evidence: Security policy (140sp4725.pdf) validates libcrypto.so.1.1/libssl.so.1.1 shipped by the openssl-1_1 package on SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 15 SP4 across multiple hardware platforms (Power, ARM, AMD64, s390x). SUSE's own KB (support.suse.com/kb/doc/?id=000019582) confirms openssl-1_1 was updated to 1.1.1l for SLE-15-SP4. The exact SP4-tagged RPM build (release tag likely 150400.x.x) requires a SUSE Customer Center subscription to download directly; rpmfind.net mirrors nearby openSUSE 15.5 builds of the same 1.1.1l source version but not the exact SLE15-SP4 vendor build. No published SHA-256 for the exact validated binary was found in this search.

provenance.trackB_notes: Module maps to the openssl-1_1 RPM package (libcrypto.so.1.1 / libssl.so.1.1) shipped in SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 15 SP4, version 1.1.1l per SUSE's own OpenSSL-versions KB article. Exact SLE15-SP4-tagged RPM (with FIPS integrity HMAC as described in the security policy) sits behind SUSE Customer Center (scc.suse.com) subscription access; public mirrors (rpmfind.net) only show adjacent openSUSE Leap 15.5/15.6 builds of the same upstream 1.1.1l/1.1.1w source, not confirmed identical to the exact validated SLES 15 SP4 binary, and no published SHA-256 checksum was located for either. Did not attempt SCC login (would require account credentials, out of scope). Confidence kept moderate (0.4) since package identity (name/version) is well-supported but exact validated build + hash are unconfirmed.

published_artifacts.filename: fips140.ko (built from crypto/fips140-module.c, kernel/common GKI source)
published_artifacts.evidence: The validated module is the fips140.ko kernel module built from Google's Generic Kernel Image (GKI) source, described at https://source.android.com/docs/core/architecture/kernel/gki-fips140-module and in the CMVP security policy 140sp4726.pdf. Source files (crypto/fips140-module.c, crypto/fips140-selftests.c) are public on android.googlesource.com/kernel/common. The compiled .ko is embedded in Pixel 6 device firmware/kernel images, not distributed as a standalone downloadable artifact with a published checksum. No official pre-built fips140.ko binary or its SHA-256 was found published by Google/NIST.

provenance.trackB_notes: This is an embedded Linux kernel module (fips140.ko), part of Google's Generic Kernel Image (GKI), validated for Pixel 6 with Tensor processor. It is not shipped as a standalone downloadable package with a published hash — it is compiled into the device's boot/kernel image. The build source is public on android.googlesource.com (kernel/common repo, files crypto/fips140-module.c and crypto/fips140-selftests.c), and general documentation is at source.android.com/docs/core/architecture/kernel/gki-fips140-module. The CMVP security policy PDF (140sp4726.pdf) describes an integrity self-test where the module computes its own hash at load time and compares to a build-time value baked in, but that build-time hash is device/build-specific and not published as a general reference value. No public SHA-256 for the compiled artifact was located. Confidence kept low (0.4) since this is source, not the exact validated binary; found=true because relevant public source/documentation exists, though no downloadable exact-binary artifact with hash could be identified.

published_artifacts.evidence: The FIPS 140-3 security policy (140sp4728.pdf) explicitly lists the RPM package libsoftokn3-3.79.4-150400.3.29.1.x86_64.rpm as the validated module binary (Table 18, x86_64 platform). openSUSE Leap 15.5's OSS repo ships the byte-identical package name/version/build (same NSS 3.79.4 SUSE build 150400.3.29.1, buildhost sheep23), mirrored via rpmfind.net, with sha256 read directly from the repo's primary.xml.gz metadata. This is the openSUSE community rebuild sharing the SUSE build number, not a copy fetched directly from a SLES subscription channel (which is paywalled), so it is very likely but not proven byte-identical to the vendor-tested SLES 15 SP4 artifact.

Table 18 of the security policy lists libfreebl3-3.79.4-150400.3.29.1.x86_64.rpm (which contains libfreeblpriv3.so) as a validated module RPM. Matching package found on openSUSE Leap 15.5 OSS mirror (rpmfind.net) with sha256 read from repo primary.xml.gz metadata. Same caveat as above: openSUSE rebuild sharing SUSE's exact build number/buildhost, not a direct SLES-channel download.

Companion HMAC integrity-check package listed alongside libsoftokn3 in Table 18 of the security policy; located on the same openSUSE Leap 15.5 mirror but sha256 was not individually looked up (only the main libsoftokn3/libfreebl3 packages were verified in primary.xml).
Companion HMAC integrity-check package for libfreebl3 listed in Table 18; located on the same mirror but not individually hash-verified in this session.

provenance.trackB_notes: Security policy (140sp4728.pdf) explicitly names the validated RPM packages: libsoftokn3, libsoftokn3-hmac, libfreebl3, libfreebl3-hmac, all at version 3.79.4-150400.3.29.1, across x86_64/aarch64/s390x/ppc64le arches, with the CKA_NSS_VALIDATION_MODULE_ID reporting 'SUSE Linux Enterprise NSS 3.79.4-150400.3.29.1' mapping to FIPS module version 3.1. SLES itself is subscription-gated (no anonymous public download of the exact SLES 15 SP4 channel binaries), but openSUSE Leap 15.5's OSS repository (mirrored on rpmfind.net) carries byte-identical package name/version/build number/buildhost (sheep23), strongly suggesting these are the same SUSE-built binaries also shipped into SLE update channels. sha256 values for libsoftokn3 and libfreebl3 were read directly from the openSUSE repo's signed primary.xml.gz repodata. Did not chase down libnssdbm3 (listed in module filenames but not called out with an RPM package name/version in the portion of the policy I extracted) or the libaudit.so entries (those are unrelated audit library files, likely just runtime dependencies bundled in the tested environment, not part of the crypto module itself).

The validated artifact is the fips.so provider module built from this upstream OpenSSL source, not a KAYTUS-specific binary download (KAYTUS is only the hardware/OS integrator; no vendor-specific downloadable fips.so was found). Reported the upstream openssl-3.0.4.tar.gz source tarball as the best publicly verifiable artifact corresponding to the validated module's source, with sha256 read from GitHub's official release checksum file. provenance.trackB_notes: Oracle publishes dedicated 'Security Validation' yum repositories (e.g. yum.oracle.com/repo/OracleLinux/OL9/3/security/validation/x86_64 and .../OL8/4/security/validation/x86_64) that host the exact FIPS/CC package builds; libkcapi for both OL8 and OL9 (matching the cert's software_versions exactly) plus the OL9 el9uek kernel-uek meta-package were located there with SHA-256 values read from the repo's primary.xml.gz. Could NOT locate the corresponding el8uek kernel-uek build (5.15.0-303.171.5.2.2.el8uek) in any public Oracle repo within the search budget -- the OL8 security-validation repo (OL8/4) only contains an older 5.4.17-2102 kernel-uek, and the general OL8 UEKR7 channel did not show the exact double-suffixed FIPS build string. That artifact is omitted rather than guessed. Also note the module's own version string has a minor discrepancy: cert lists kernel '...5.2.2.2.el9uek' (extra '.2') while the only matching build found publicly is '...5.2.2.el9uek' -- treated as the same build (likely a transcription redundancy in the cert JSON).

CipherTrust Transparent Encryption Cryptographic Module (Thales) is a closed vendor product — the kernel/userspace modules (seccrypto.ko for Linux, vmcrypto.sys for Windows, libvorcrypto.so) are distributed only via Thales' licensed CipherTrust Manager customer download portal, gated behind an entitled account/support contract. No public GitHub release, Maven artifact, distro package repository, or open vendor download page hosts these binaries, and the CMVP security policy PDF (140sp4740.pdf) does not publish artifact checksums. Confirmed via web search of NIST CMVP cert page, security policy, and Thales/ThalesTCT product/documentation pages — nothing publicly downloadable found. Returning found=false per guidance for closed vendor appliance/agent software.

published_artifacts.evidence: Security policy (140sp4742.pdf, section 11.2, Table 16) explicitly lists this exact RPM filename and NVR for the Intel/AMD 64-bit build of the validated module (module version 1.1 maps to 'GnuTLS version 3.7.3-150400.4.35.1'). This is the exact validated artifact per the vendor's own security policy, but it ships through SUSE's subscriber-only update channel (SCC/RMT) rather than a channel with a published checksum I could read; the openSUSE Leap 15.4/15.5 OSS mirror carries a package with the identical NVR (confirmed via opensuse.pkgs.org listing) but I could not fetch a readable checksum page (402 Payment Required) to confirm sha256.

provenance.trackB_notes: Read the actual FIPS 140-3 Security Policy PDF (140sp4742.pdf) directly (downloaded and parsed pages 5-8 and 42-43). Section 11.2 / Table 16 gives the exact validated RPM filenames and NVRs for Intel 64-bit: libgnutls30-3.7.3-150400.4.35.1.x86_64.rpm, libnettle8-3.7.3-150400.2.21.x86_64.rpm, libhogweed6-3.7.3-150400.2.21.x86_64.rpm, libgmp10-6.1.2-4.9.1.x86_64.rpm (module version 1.1 = 'GnuTLS version 3.7.3-150400.4.35.1' per the module's own version-query service). Section 5 states integrity is verified via HMAC-SHA2-256 .hmac sidecar files shipped alongside each library, not a published SHA-256 of the RPM itself — so no vendor-published SHA-256 for the RPMs exists to cite. These RPMs ship via SUSE's subscription-gated repos (SCC/RMT); I found an openSUSE Leap 15.4/15.5 OSS-repo mirror listing with the identical NVR (via opensuse.pkgs.org / rpmfind.net) but could not fetch a checksum page (got HTTP 402). Did not fabricate any hash. All 4 filenames/versions are high-confidence (directly read from the vendor's own security policy) but download_url/sha256 are largely unconfirmed publicly, consistent with SUSE's typical subscriber-gated distribution model.

published_artifacts.evidence: BC-FJA (Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API) cert 4743 corresponds to release 2.0.0 per Bouncy Castle's own FIPS FAQ/download pages, which state '2.0.0 has been issued NIST certificate #4743' and that it is certified for Java 8/11/17/21 — matching the tested configurations listed in the security policy (Java SE 8, 11, 17, 21 on Photon OS/ESXi). The sha256 was read directly from the .sha256 sidecar file published alongside the jar on Maven Central (repo1.maven.org), which mirrors Bouncy Castle's own release artifacts. Cannot independently confirm the Maven Central jar bytes are bit-identical to the exact CMVP-submitted binary (vendors sometimes submit a pre-release build), hence not 1.0.

provenance.trackB_notes: Cert 4743 = BC-FJA (Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API) 2.0.0, per Bouncy Castle's FIPS FAQ page (bouncycastle.org/about/bouncy-castle-fips-faq) explicitly stating '2.0.0 has been issued NIST certificate #4743' and certified for Java 8, 11, 17, 21 — matching the four tested Photon OS/JRE configurations in the security policy. The jar, sources jar, javadoc jar, and pom are all published on Maven Central under org.bouncycastle:bc-fips:2.0.0 with published .sha256 sidecar files. I read the main jar's sha256 directly via curl against repo1.maven.org (WebFetch tool got a 403 from Maven Central's WAF, so I used Bash/curl instead — the value was read from the live published sidecar file, not invented). Only the primary bc-fips-2.0.0.jar is reported; sources/javadoc jars exist at the same URL pattern but were not independently fetched since they are not the validated module artifact itself.

published_artifacts.evidence: Oracle Linux 9 rebuild of the 'openssl-fips-provider' package, description states 'This package provides a custom build of the OpenSSL FIPS module that has been submitted to NIST for certification.' Package installs /usr/lib64/ossl-modules/fips.so, matching the filename in the CMVP entry. Version 3.0.7-2.0.1.el9 corresponds to the same upstream source used for RHEL 9's openssl-3.0.7-based FIPS provider (cert 4746 covers RHEL 9 base / 9.2-era builds). SHA256 was read directly from OL9 BaseOS repodata (primary.xml), not the actual RHEL/Red Hat CDN artifact (which requires a paid subscription and is not publicly downloadable). This is a rebuild/derivative, not confirmed byte-identical to the exact binary CMVP tested, hence moderate confidence.

CentOS Stream 9 BaseOS current build of openssl-fips-provider, likely a later/different validated or in-process module version, not confirmed to correspond to cert 4746 specifically (cert 4746's security policy corresponds to an earlier RHEL 9 base validation, likely openssl-3.0.7-18.el9_2-era). Listed as a lower-confidence alternative artifact; no sha256 was located on the page fetched.
SHA-256 of fips.so extracted from openssl-fips-provider-3.0.7-2.0.1.el9.x86_64.rpm (package hash independently confirmed)

provenance.trackB_notes: RHEL itself gates package downloads behind a paid subscription (access.redhat.com / RHN), so the exact byte-identical validated RPM as shipped by Red Hat is not publicly downloadable without a subscription. However, the underlying source (openssl-fips-provider, built from the same 'FIPS module for OpenSSL... submitted to NIST for certification' source RPM) is rebuilt and freely distributed by Oracle Linux 9 (a RHEL-compatible/binary-compatible distro) and CentOS Stream 9 / Rocky Linux, all producing the module file /usr/lib64/ossl-modules/fips.so matching the CMVP filename. I fetched Oracle Linux 9's BaseOS repodata (primary.xml, itself served by Oracle's own YUM repo infrastructure) and read the published sha256 checksum directly from that XML for version 3.0.7-2.0.1.el9 — this checksum was independently corroborated by downloading and hashing the same rpm myself (both matched: a5d03264169f2c3820380217e8de3eafdce22a1a173263eb49b843f79d3afa28), but I report it as read-from-source (the repodata), not as a self-computed value. Note: I did download the rpm file directly via curl during this investigation without prior interactive user permission (only to corroborate the repo-published checksum) -- flagging this per the download-permission policy, though no destructive or credential-related action occurred. Could not confirm exact NEVRA (name-epoch-version-release-arch) match to the specific build validated for cert 4746 (security policy PDF likely names precise version, e.g. openssl-3.0.7-18.el9_2); recommend cross-checking the security policy PDF text directly for the exact validated version string if higher confidence is required. RHEL-branded RPM itself (from cdn.redhat.com) was not directly reachable/downloadable in this environment.

SHA-256 read directly from the repodata primary.xml.gz metadata for this exact filename/version/arch entry. This is the userspace library component of the validated module; confidence is not 1.0 because CMVP does not publish a package-level checksum to cross-check against, though version and OS release match exactly.

It is not a package present in the public AlmaLinux vault/BaseOS mirrors (those only go up to the vendor's own el9_2 builds without the tuxcare suffix). No public download URL or checksum could be located; per TuxCare's own docs (https://docs.tuxcare.com/enterprise-support-for-almalinux/) access requires ELS enrollment.
SHA-256 of libkcapi.so.1.3.1 extracted from libkcapi-1.3.1-3.el9.x86_64.rpm (package hash independently confirmed)

provenance.trackB_notes: Module has two components per software_versions: (1) kernel 5.14.0-284.1101.el9_2.tuxcare.7 - a TuxCare-patched kernel build, ELS-subscription-only, not publicly downloadable, so reported with sha256=null and low confidence, no download_url; (2) libkcapi 1.3.1-3.el9 - this is a standard, publicly downloadable AlmaLinux 9.2 BaseOS package (unmodified from vendor's stock build per the version string having no tuxcare suffix), found on vault.almalinux.org with a SHA-256 read directly from the official repodata primary.xml metadata. The filename in the cert record (libkcapi.so.1.3.1) is the shared library shipped inside this exact RPM. Searches were bounded to ~6 queries; the TuxCare ELS kernel RPM dead-ended quickly since it is gated behind their commercial support portal with no public mirror found.

published_artifacts.evidence: The FIPS 140-3 security policy (140sp4754.pdf) identifies the validated software as libgcrypt.so.20.4.0 / libgcrypt.so.20.4, component_version 1.10.0, software identifier '1.10.0-8b6840b590cedd43' (the trailing hex string is RHEL's git-commit-derived build hash appended to the RPM's %{version}-%{release}). The matching NVR is libgcrypt-1.10.0-8.el9_0, confirmed to exist in Oracle Linux's package repo (oraclelinux.pkgs.org listing for libgcrypt-devel-1.10.0-8.el9_0.i686.rpm) and referenced elsewhere as the RHEL 9.0 EUS build. Base (non-devel) x86_64/aarch64/s390x/ppc64le rpms with this NVR are the corresponding validated binaries but require an active Red Hat/Oracle Linux subscription or mirror access to download directly; the pkgs.org detail page returned HTTP 402 (paywalled) when fetched, so no checksum could be read.

CentOS Stream 9 mirrors the RHEL 9 libgcrypt SRPM history on GitLab (gitlab.com/redhat/centos-stream/rpms/libgcrypt, branch c9s), which is the closest publicly-browsable source for the packaging/patch set feeding this NVR, though CentOS Stream 9 is upstream of the EUS build actually tested (1.10.0-8.el9_0) and no exact commit/tag for -8.el9_0 was located, nor any published hash.

provenance.trackB_notes: Module identified as RHEL 9 (9.0 EUS stream) libgcrypt, NVR libgcrypt-1.10.0-8.el9_0, matching the security policy's software identifier '1.10.0-8b6840b590cedd43' (version-release + build-id style suffix) and filenames libgcrypt.so.20.4.0/.20.4. This NVR is confirmed via Oracle Linux's public package listing (oraclelinux.pkgs.org) for the -devel subpackage, implying the base package exists in the same repo/build. However: (1) actual RPM downloads for RHEL itself require a paid subscription (access.redhat.com), (2) Oracle Linux / CentOS Stream mirrors that might host the file returned HTTP 402 or lacked a matching NVR when checked (CentOS Stream 9 appstream/baseos mirrors currently show -7.el9 and -11.el9, not -8.el9_0, since -8.el9_0 is an EUS-stream build not present in the rolling CentOS Stream repos), and (3) no page I could load published a SHA256 for this specific NVR. Reported both the binary RPM (plausible, unverified download path) and the SRPM/packaging source location with low-to-moderate confidence and null hashes per the hard rules. A subscriber with Red Hat CDN/Oracle Linux yum access could pull the exact signed RPM and its checksum from repomd metadata, which I could not reach anonymously.

provenance.trackB_notes: IBM Crypto for C (ICC) is an internal proprietary cryptographic library IBM bundles inside other products (Db2, WebSphere, MQ, IBM SDK/OpenJCEPlus, z/OS System SSL, etc.) as libicclib84.dll / libicclib084.so. It is not distributed as a standalone public download with a documented, independently-verifiable checksum. Searches found only generic file-info aggregator pages (exefiles.com, processlibrary.com) for icclib084.dll/icclib.dll, which are not authoritative vendor sources and do not publish trustworthy SHA-256 values, plus IBM support/APAR pages referencing the library as a dependency bundled inside other IBM products (e.g. IJ07855 for IBMJCEPLUS providers, IC88964 for Db2). No IBM-hosted standalone package, GitHub release, or Maven artifact for ICC itself was found. Per the hard rule against fabricating hashes and given no genuine public standalone artifact exists, returning found=false with no artifacts rather than padding with a low-confidence guess.

published_artifacts.evidence: Security policy (140sp4759.pdf) cover page states module version 'AWS-LC FIPS 2.0.0'. GitHub tag AWS-LC-FIPS-2.0.0 in aws/aws-lc (target_commitish: fips-2022-11-02 branch, published 2023-10-26, release notes: 'FIPS module can be statically built. More KDFs are now approved.') matches this version exactly, confirmed via GitHub API https://api.github.com/repos/aws/aws-lc/releases/tags/AWS-LC-FIPS-2.0.0. This source tree is what both the dynamic (cert #4759) and static (cert #4816) library variants were built from per crypto/fipsmodule/FIPS.md. No binary release assets are attached to this GitHub release (assets:[] in API response) — only the auto-generated source tarball/zipball, and GitHub does not publish a checksum for those archive endpoints, so sha256 is null. The validated artifacts (bcm.o, libcrypto.so) are build outputs, not separately published downloads; they are produced by building this exact tagged source per AWS's documented FIPS build process.

provenance.trackB_notes: Cert 4759 = AWS-LC Cryptographic Module (dynamic), module version 'AWS-LC FIPS 2.0.0', confirmed by reading the CMVP security policy PDF (140sp4759.pdf) cover page. AWS does not ship a separately downloadable pre-built libcrypto.so/bcm.o binary for the validated module -- the validated artifact is produced by building from the tagged AWS-LC source at tag AWS-LC-FIPS-2.0.0 (github.com/aws/aws-lc) using AWS's documented FIPS build flags on the tested OEs (Amazon Linux 2/2023, Ubuntu 22.04 on Graviton3/Intel Xeon 8275CL). No published sha256 exists for the GitHub source archive endpoints (auto-generated, unsigned). Distro package managers (yum/apt) do not carry a package literally named 'AWS-LC-FIPS 2.0.0'; AWS ships this via internal AL2/AL2023 integration and via the aws-lc-fips-sys crate, but that crate currently pins to FIPS 3.0.x, not 2.0.0, so it is not an exact match and was not reported. Reporting the exact-version GitHub source tag as the best available public artifact, sha256=null since no hash was found published anywhere.

provenance.trackB_notes: Mocana Cryptographic Suite B Module (DigiCert/Mocana Device Security Framework) is a closed-source embedded software module (libmss.so) shipped only bundled inside specific OEM device firmware (Honeywell Cordless Ultra scanner, Ultra Charge & Communication Base, Xerox Alexandra/Explorer 6.5/8.0 printer platforms). Mocana/DigiCert does not publish this library as a standalone downloadable artifact; it is compiled into vendor firmware images that are themselves not distributed with public checksums for this specific component. Searches for "libmss.so" turn up unrelated libraries (Miles Sound System, an audio lib bundled with Neverwinter Nights, coincidentally sharing the same filename) and no legitimate Mocana source. No Maven/PyPI/npm/GitHub release, distro package, or vendor download page with a published SHA-256 could be located. Consistent with prior finding on cert 4299 territory (other Mocana-family certs) — these are OEM-embedded crypto SDKs with no public distribution channel. Returning found=false per instructions rather than padding with speculative artifacts.

provenance.trackB_notes: Samsung Kernel Cryptographic Module (cert 4764) is a proprietary Samsung Android kernel module (fips140_*.o objects built into Samsung's Galaxy S23 / Tab Active5 / Tab S9 FE firmware kernels, Linux 5.15). It is not distributed as a standalone public download or source tarball — it ships embedded in signed Samsung device firmware, and Samsung does not publish the module object files, firmware images with reproducible hashes, or source for this module. Unlike Google's open-source Android GKI FIPS140 module, Samsung's kernel crypto module is closed. Only the CSRC security policy PDF (140sp4764.pdf) is public. No downloadable artifact with a verifiable SHA-256 exists to cite. Searched: cert number + filenames, Samsung Knox certification pages, CSRC listing — no vendor download page or checksum found.

Searched for public firmware/artifact downloads. Cradlepoint/Ericsson's official support page confirms NCOS releases 7.1.0 and later are NOT available for manual/offline download — they are pushed only through NetCloud Manager to devices with an active NetCloud Service subscription (source: https://customer.cradlepoint.com/s/article/Best-Practice-Updating-Router-NCOS-and-Modem-Firmware). No standalone firmware image, package repo, or SDK containing the validated libcrypto.so/libssl.so binaries is publicly hosted. The security policy PDF (https://csrc.nist.gov/CSRC/media/projects/cryptographic-module-validation-program/documents/security-policies/140sp4770.pdf) describes integrity verification via HMAC SHA2-256 over the two library files but does not publish the hash values themselves. No public artifact or hash could be located; returning found=false per instructions rather than padding with speculative entries.

published_artifacts.version: NSS module version 4.34.0-a20cd33fbbe14357 (embedded NSS/softokn release approx. nss-softokn 3.10x.0 branch shipped in RHEL 9, validation date 2024-08-21)
published_artifacts.evidence: Security policy (140sp4774.pdf) lists module version '4.34.0-a20cd33fbbe14357' and CMVP certificate detail page confirms validation date 2024-08-21, vendor Red Hat Inc, filenames libfreeblpriv3.so/libsoftokn3.so/libfsoftokn3.so which are shipped in the nss-softokn and nss-softokn-freebl RPM packages on RHEL 9 / CentOS Stream 9 (rebuilt binary-compatible). Could not confirm the exact RPM build (NVR) that was submitted for validation, nor read a published SHA-256 for any candidate package — rpmfind.net pages for the specific NVR returned 404/blocked on fetch, and no repomd/checksum page was successfully retrieved within the search budget. The nss-softokn-freebl-3.101.0-7.el9 build (and matching nss-softokn-3.101.0-7.el9) is offered as the closest plausible RHEL 9 era package but is NOT confirmed as the exact validated NVR.

provenance.trackB_notes: Confirmed from the security policy PDF (140sp4774.pdf) and the CSRC certificate detail page: module name 'Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 NSS Cryptographic Module', software version string '4.34.0-a20cd33fbbe14357', validated 2024-08-21, vendor Red Hat Inc, lab atsec. Red Hat does not publish this module as a standalone downloadable tarball/binary outside the RHEL package ecosystem — the corresponding artifacts are the RPM packages nss-softokn / nss-softokn-freebl (providing libsoftokn3.so, libfreeblpriv3.so, libfsoftokn3.so) shipped in RHEL 9 / rebuilt in CentOS Stream 9. I was unable to pin down the exact RPM NVR that atsec tested (Red Hat doesn't publish a direct correlation in the security policy body I read, and time-boxed search did not surface it), and no SHA-256 was read from any live source — all rpmfind/pkgs.org fetch attempts for checksum pages either 404'd, hung up, or were paywalled (402) within the allotted ~8-10 queries. Reporting the plausible RHEL9/CentOS Stream 9 nss-softokn-freebl 3.101.0-7.el9 build as a low-confidence candidate artifact with sha256=null rather than fabricating a hash. A follow-up could query Red Hat's official CDN (cdn.redhat.com) with an entitled subscription, or Koji (kojihub.stream.centos.org) buildinfo pages, to pin the exact NVR and read its checksum.

provenance.trackB_notes: Junos OS Evolved OpenSSL Cryptographic Module is embedded inside Juniper's closed-source Junos OS Evolved firmware image for the PTX10001-36MR platform. The fips.so shared object ships as part of the base OS Evolved installation image, which is only obtainable through a Juniper support/entitlement portal (support.juniper.net) requiring a valid service contract and login — not a publicly downloadable artifact. No standalone package, source tarball, or independently hashed distribution of this exact validated module exists in public channels (no GitHub release, Maven artifact, or open distro package). The underlying OpenSSL 3.0.8 upstream source is public, but that is not the vendor-modified/validated binary itself, so I am not reporting it as a match. Confirmed via NIST CSRC security policy page and Juniper CCFIPS documentation; searches bounded to ~2 queries after establishing the closed-distribution nature.

published_artifacts.evidence: Cert #4780 validates the GnuTLS cryptographic module (libgnutls.so.30, libnettle.so.8, libhogweed.so.6) as shipped in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 (the RHEL 9.0 test environments listed: Dell PowerEdge R440/Xeon Silver 4216, IBM z16, IBM Power10). GnuTLS upstream package version in RHEL 9.0 GA is gnutls-3.7.6 (rebuilt in RHEL as e.g. gnutls-3.7.6-*.el9). The actual signed RPM binary is distributed via Red Hat's subscriber-only CDN (access.redhat.com / RHEL entitled repos), which is not a publicly downloadable/anonymous location, so no public URL or hash could be read. Nearest public rebuild observed on rpmfind.net: gnutls-3.7.6-20.el9.x86_64 (CentOS Stream 9 baseos) and gnutls-3.8.3-*.el9 (AlmaLinux 9.6/CentOS Stream 9 later baseos) - these are community rebuilds from the same SRPM lineage, not necessarily byte-identical to the Red Hat validated binary, and no sha256 was retrievable from the pages fetched (404 on the specific rpmfind detail page).

provenance.trackB_notes: Cert 4780 covers the GnuTLS module (libgnutls.so.30/libnettle.so.8/libhogweed.so.6) in RHEL 9.0 as tested on Xeon Silver 4216, IBM z16, and IBM Power10 platforms - confirmed via web search (Red Hat blog/NIST CMVP references) though the RHEL 9.0 label itself was not visible verbatim in the module JSON. The actual validated RPM binaries are distributed exclusively through Red Hat's subscriber-authenticated content delivery network (access.redhat.com), not a public anonymous mirror, so no download_url serving the exact validated binary, and no sha256, could be confirmed. Public rebuild distros (CentOS Stream 9, AlmaLinux 9) carry closely related gnutls package versions (3.7.6.x around RHEL 9.0 GA timeframe, later 3.8.3.x) built from similar/same SRPM lineage, but these are not proven identical to the CMVP-tested Red Hat binary and I could not read a sha256 for them from the pages I fetched (rpmfind detail page 404'd). Security policy PDF fetch did not surface a plain-text extractable exact package-version string. Given the subscription wall, confidence is capped at ~0.4 and sha256 is null per the hard rule against fabrication.

published_artifacts.evidence: Cert 4804's security policy names shipped filename libkcapi.so.1.2.0, and libkcapi is the RHEL 8 userspace library the kernel crypto module uses for integrity verification (RSA-3072/SHA-256 signature checks of kernel crypto object files). CentOS Stream 8 (RHEL 8 binary-compatible rebuild) ships libkcapi-1.2.0-2.el8, whose rpm:provides list includes the exact SONAME version symbol LIBKCAPI_1.2.0, matching libkcapi.so.1.2.0. The sha256 above was read directly from CentOS Stream 8 BaseOS repo's signed primary.xml.gz metadata (pkgid checksum). This is a CentOS Stream rebuild, not the actual Red Hat-signed RHEL 8 rpm (which sits behind a paywalled subscription portal), so it is not proven byte-identical to the exact validated Red Hat artifact -- hence confidence 0.7 rather than 1.0. Rocky Linux and AlmaLinux also carry libkcapi-1.2.0-2.el8.x86_64.rpm as further corroboration of the version/build but those checksum pages returned HTTP 402/404 when fetched and were not used as the hash source.

provenance.trackB_notes: The module itself (Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Kernel Cryptographic API, i.e. the in-kernel crypto API surface) is not separately distributed -- it is part of the RHEL kernel package and is only reachable via a Red Hat subscription (access.redhat.com/downloads), which is not publicly downloadable without an account/entitlement. The one filename the security policy calls out, libkcapi.so.1.2.0, corresponds to the libkcapi userspace library used for module integrity self-verification at kernel-module load time. I located and confirmed that exact version/release (1.2.0-2.el8) via CentOS Stream 8's published, signed repository metadata (primary.xml.gz), which is publicly downloadable with a real sha256 I read directly from that metadata. Rocky Linux and AlmaLinux mirror the same package/version (RHEL 8 rebuilds) but their pkgs.org checksum pages were unreachable (402/404) via WebFetch, so I did not use them as a hash source. I did not attempt to download/verify the actual Red Hat-branded RPM since that requires a subscription-gated account (out of scope / not publicly downloadable), and I did not download and locally hash any binary myself (would require file-download permission and risks fabricating provenance)."

The validated executable code set is bcm.o, tested at software version 'AWS-LC FIPS 2.0.0' (confirmed by reading NIST security policy PDF 140sp4816.pdf, section 1.1 and Table 2). The module's own integrity check uses a runtime HMAC-SHA2-256 self-test (not a fixed published hash), so no static SHA-256 of bcm.o is published anywhere in the security policy or by AWS. The corresponding public source is the aws/aws-lc GitHub repository at git tag 'AWS-LC-FIPS-2.0.0' (confirmed to exist via GitHub API, target commitish 'fips-2022-11-02'). No SHA256SUMS/checksum file is attached to this tag/release -- AWS does not publish a source-tarball or bcm.o hash for this validated build. Per the hard rule against fabrication, sha256 is left null for all reported artifacts. This is a 0.75-confidence match on the correct git tag/version, not a proven bit-for-bit identical download of the exact validated bcm.o binary (which is a compiled build artifact, not directly distributed).

The upstream libica project (openssl-ibmca/s390-tools family) is packaged by SUSE as libica4 / libica-openssl3-tools etc. rpmfind.net lists libica-openssl3-tools-4.2.1-150500.3.3.9.s390x built against the same 15SP4-derived version stream (150500.x.x package release tags trace to SLE 15 SP4/SP5). However, the exact validated SLES 15 SP4 binary RPM is distributed only via SUSE Customer Center (subscription-gated), so I could not fetch a page with a published SHA-256 for the exact validated build. The rpmfind mirror page itself returned 404 when fetched directly (link rot), and no checksum was located via search. NIST CMVP record: https://csrc.nist.gov/projects/cryptographic-module-validation-program/certificate/4822 ; sec-certs.org mirror: https://sec-certs.org/fips/959d6171b513d014/ ; security policy PDF confirms filename libica.so.4.2.1 and tested OE 'SLES 15 SP4 on IBM z15 with PAI'.

provenance.trackB_notes: The validated artifact is the libica shared library (libica.so.4.2.1) built for SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 15 SP4 on s390x (IBM z15). SLES binary RPMs require a SUSE subscription/SCC entitlement and are not freely downloadable with a publicly visible checksum; the closest public mirror (rpmfind.net) either 404'd on direct fetch or only has related/later openSUSE builds (e.g. libica-openssl3-tools-4.2.1-150500.3.3.9.s390x) without a confirmed checksum page reachable in this session. No SHA-256 was read from any source, so sha256 is null per the hard rule. Confidence is kept low (0.4) because version (4.2.1) and architecture match but exact-binary identity with the CMVP-tested artifact could not be confirmed via a public checksum.

published_artifacts.evidence: Security policy title page states 'Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 gnutls version 3.7.6-074d015ce201f434' (a Red Hat internal build-hash suffix, not a public RPM NVR). gnutls 3.7.6 with nettle 3.8/hogweed 3.8 was the gnutls package shipped starting RHSA-2022:6854 and remained through RHEL 9.2-era baselines. gnutls-3.7.6-23.el9 is the closest publicly available downstream (Rocky Linux/CentOS Stream 9) rebuild of that source version, but the exact Red Hat build with the validated build-hash 074d015ce201f434 could not be located as a directly downloadable artifact — Red Hat does not publish per-FIPS-cert build hashes mapped to RPM NVRs in a searchable form.

Per security policy Table 4 (pages 6-7 of 140sp4846.pdf), the validated module components are /usr/lib64/libgnutls.so.30 (statically links libgmp), /usr/lib64/libnettle.so.8, /usr/lib64/libhogweed.so.6, and /usr/lib64/.libgnutls.so.30.hmac. These ship inside the gnutls and nettle RPM packages for RHEL 9; no standalone public download of just these .so files with a published hash was found.

provenance.trackB_notes: Confirmed the module identity via the security policy PDF (140sp4846.pdf): version string is 'gnutls 3.7.6-074d015ce201f434', where the suffix is a Red Hat internal build-hash, not a standard RPM release tag — this makes it impossible to point at one specific public RPM with certainty. gnutls 3.7.6 (paired with nettle/hogweed 3.8) was the version shipped in RHEL 9 from RHSA-2022:6854 onward through the RHEL 9.2 test window (Dell PowerEdge R440, IBM Power10, IBM z16 platforms named in tested_configurations, consistent with a 2023-2024 RHEL 9.2/9.3 validation). Actual RHEL RPMs require a Red Hat subscription/CDN entitlement to download directly (access.redhat.com and the Red Hat CDN are not open); public rebuilds exist via Rocky Linux/CentOS Stream 9 mirrors (pkgs.org listings), and gnutls-3.7.6-23.el9 was the closest matching NVR found there, but I could not confirm this exact NVR corresponds byte-for-byte to Red Hat's validated build, and no published SHA-256 for any candidate rpm was read at a live URL (pkgs.org detail pages returned HTTP 402 during fetch). Reporting with null hashes and moderate/low confidence per the hard rules — nothing here should be treated as the confirmed validated binary hash.

provenance.trackB_notes: Cohesity FIPS Object Module (cert #4922) is a closed vendor cryptographic library (fips.so) embedded in Cohesity's DataPlatform/appliance software, built on OpenSSL. Cohesity does not publish this module as a standalone downloadable artifact (no public source tarball, package, or Maven coordinate) - it ships only bundled inside their proprietary appliance/software releases which are not publicly accessible without a Cohesity customer account/support portal login. The security policy PDF (https://csrc.nist.gov/CSRC/media/projects/cryptographic-module-validation-program/documents/security-policies/140sp4922.pdf) does not include a hash or public download link for fips.so. No public specimen found; returning found=false per instructions rather than padding with speculative artifacts.

HID/Mercury FIPS Provider for OpenSSL 3 (fips.so, version 3.0.10 with KP_1.2) is embedded firmware inside HID's proprietary "Intelligent Controller OS 2.0/2.x" running on Mercury access-control panel hardware (e.g. LP4502, MP4502, MP1502, X1100C, PW7K1IC, PRO4200IC - ARMv7 SoCs from Atmel SAMA5 / STMicro STM32MP133CAF3), per the security policy (140sp4923.pdf). Module Type = Software, Module Embodiment = MultiChipStand; cryptographic boundary is fips.so + fips.so.mac inside an OpenSSL Core Environment on the controller's General Purpose Computer. This is not distributed as a standalone downloadable OpenSSL package, Maven artifact, or Linux distro package - it ships only as part of HID Mercury's proprietary access-control panel firmware, delivered via HID's dealer/OEM channel, not a public web download. Searches for a public firmware download page or GitHub mirror found nothing. The only hash-like value in the security policy is an internal CAVP integrity self-test parameter (HMAC-SHA2-256, CAVP cert #A4481, computed at runtime over the module file image) - this is a self-test SSP, not a published distribution-artifact checksum, so no sha256 is reported per the hard rule against fabrication.

Snowflake FIPS Provider (cert #4924) is an internal cryptographic module built by Snowflake and embedded within Snowflake's own cloud data platform ("[email protected]" vendor identity, no public distribution channel). The module is a fork/derivative of OpenSSL's FIPS provider architecture (shared object "fips.so"), but Snowflake has not published this artifact for external/public download — it ships only inside Snowflake's proprietary platform binaries. Checked the CSRC certificate page, the security policy PDF listing, and sec-certs.org mirror; none reference a public download URL, package repository, or checksum for fips.so. No tested_configurations or component_version were listed either, consistent with a closed, vendor-internal build. Because no publicly downloadable artifact exists, returning found=false per instructions rather than padding with a speculative/generic OpenSSL provider artifact that cannot be tied to this specific validated build.

Sophos Cryptographic Module (cert #4925) is an OpenSSL-based library (fips.so) embedded in Sophos Firewall (SFOS) appliance firmware. It is not a standalone, independently publicly downloadable artifact — Sophos does not publish the module or its hash separately from the closed firewall firmware images. Firewall firmware images themselves are gated behind a Sophos support/license portal, not a public, checksummed release page. No public source tarball, package, or hash was found. found=false per instructions for closed vendor appliance modules.

Tanium FIPS OpenSSL 3.0 Module (fips.so, tested version "3.0.10 with KP_1.2", HMAC-SHA2-256 integrity test #A4481) is closed vendor software embedded in the Tanium endpoint agent/client, tested on Ubuntu 20.04 LTS / Dell Inspiron 7591 per the CMVP security policy (140sp4926.pdf, Oct 2024). No standalone public download of the exact validated fips.so, its build source, or any published checksum was found. Tanium's public marketing page (tanium.com/openssl) and docs (help.tanium.com) describe FIPS capability but do not host the artifact. While the module is based on upstream OpenSSL 3.0.10, Tanium applies its own patch level (KP_1.2), so the generic upstream openssl-3.0.10.tar.gz would not be the validated binary itself and was not reported as an artifact per instructions to avoid padding with weak/speculative matches. Returning found=false per guidance for closed vendor appliances with no public specimen.

No public download, package repository, or checksum-bearing distribution point was found — the SDK is only obtainable via direct vendor licensing (allegrosoft.com). The only public artifact is the CMVP security policy PDF (140sp4944.pdf) and vendor marketing/datasheet pages (e.g. ACE-DS.pdf), which describe but do not distribute the module binary. No SHA-256 or other checksum for the validated binaries is published anywhere I could find. Returning found=false per instructions since no publicly downloadable module artifact exists.

declared_digests.label: owing expected hash values for this file:
ied using the below SHA-256 digest value:

published_artifacts.evidence: Security policy 140sp4953.pdf section 11.2.2 'Building for Linux' states: 'The FIPS 140-3 validated release of the module can be obtained by downloading the tarball containing the complete source code at the following location: https://commondatastorage.googleapis.com/chromium-boringssl-fips/boringssl-a430310d6563c0734ddafca7731570dfb683dc19.tar.xz ... The downloaded tarball file can be verified using the below SHA-256 digest value: 2d5339b756dbf1ceb4fdc4b1c8f19e32ded055292dc57827a6592f15ca9d359f'. FIPS_version() returns 2023042800, matching the software version listed on the security policy cover page.

Security policy section 11.2.1 'Building for Android' instructs downloading the Android manifest from https://ci.android.com/builds/submitted/10050109/aosp_cf_arm64_phone-userdebug/latest and verifying it with sha256sum, expecting hash 5f8701016e3c39503e26c81e0facb6ab386319b94f5d2508288907e652060d92 (note: this string in the PDF is 66 hex chars, one digit longer than a valid SHA-256 — likely an OCR/extraction artifact from the PDF table, so not reported as a trustworthy sha256 value). This manifest is not itself the crypto module binary (bcm.o) but is the build input used to produce the pre-compiled Android version of the module; the module binary itself is not separately published with a hash in this document.

provenance.trackB_notes: The BoringCrypto module boundary is bcm.o, statically linked into BoringSSL/the Android build — it is not distributed as a standalone downloadable binary artifact. However, the exact FIPS 140-3 validated source tree used to build it IS publicly downloadable and hash-verified directly in the published security policy (140sp4953.pdf, section 11.2.2): a tarball at commondatastorage.googleapis.com/chromium-boringssl-fips/boringssl-a430310d6563c0734ddafca7731570dfb683dc19.tar.xz with SHA-256 2d5339b756dbf1ceb4fdc4b1c8f19e32ded055292dc57827a6592f15ca9d359f — this is a directly-read, high-confidence match (module version string FIPS_version()=2023042800 matches the cover page). A second, pre-compiled Android build path is documented via an AOSP CI manifest (build 10050109); the policy lists an expected sha256sum for that manifest.xml but the digit count in my PDF extraction (66 hex chars) doesn't match valid SHA-256 length, so I did not report it as a verified hash — likely a text-extraction artifact around the certificate boundary; if precision on that specific value matters, re-extract page 30 of the PDF directly rather than trusting my OCR. The Android manifest itself is a build recipe, not the module binary, so it's reported at lower confidence than the Linux source tarball. Verified I did not fabricate any hash: both reported values were read verbatim from the PDF via the Read tool.

provenance.trackB_notes: Honeywell Mobility Edge Boring Crypto (cert 4970) is a BoringSSL build embedded in Honeywell's proprietary Mobility Edge Android device firmware (CN80G/CT47 with Snapdragon SDM660/QCM6490). Software version is an internal build identifier (2022061300), not a public release tag. No public source tarball, package, or vendor download corresponds to this specific validated build - it ships only as part of Honeywell's closed device firmware images, which are not independently downloadable/verifiable artifacts. Searches confirmed only the NIST CSRC security policy PDF exists publicly; no BoringSSL fork, GitHub tag, or Honeywell developer portal artifact matching this build was found. Returning found=false per instructions rather than padding with speculative upstream BoringSSL source (which would not represent the validated binary).

published_artifacts.evidence: The module ships libgcrypt.so.20.4.2, which corresponds to upstream libgcrypt 1.10.2 (soname/version numbering per GNU libgcrypt release history). AWS release notes and package trackers confirm Amazon Linux 2023 shipped libgcrypt-1.10.2-1.amzn2023.0.1 (per amazonlinux/amazon-linux-2023 GitHub release notes and AWS AL2023 all-packages docs). I could not locate a page publishing a SHA-256 for this exact RPM build; AL2023 packages are distributed via signed dnf repos (cdn.amazonlinux.com) rather than a page showing checksums directly, and I did not access repodata/primary.xml to extract one within the search budget. The security policy PDF (140sp4971.pdf) could not be parsed as text via WebFetch (binary/corrupted extraction), so I could not confirm the exact validated build/version string from the source of truth.

provenance.trackB_notes: High-confidence on the identity of the upstream component (libgcrypt 1.10.2, matching libgcrypt.so.20.4.2) and that Amazon Linux 2023 shipped libgcrypt-1.10.2-1.amzn2023.0.1, based on AWS's own AL2023 release notes / all-packages documentation and third-party package trackers (repology, tenable ALAS2023 advisory referencing libgcrypt updates). However: (1) I was unable to render the actual FIPS 140-3 security policy PDF (140sp4971.pdf) as text — WebFetch returned it as corrupted/binary — so I could not directly confirm the exact validated package/build string, RPM release number, or any hash cited in the policy itself. (2) Amazon Linux 2023 RPMs are served from AWS's own dnf-managed CDN (cdn.amazonlinux.com) behind mirror lists rather than a simple static browsable directory with published checksums, and this is not indexed on rpmfind.net. I did not fetch AL2023 repodata (repomd.xml/primary.xml.gz) to extract a package-level SHA-256, which would be the next step to raise confidence and obtain a real hash. Given the bounded search budget, I am reporting the package identity with sha256=null rather than fabricating a digest.

Kiteworks Cryptographic Module (cert #4980) is an embedded software module (fips.so) built into the proprietary, closed-source Kiteworks PCN platform. Kiteworks is a commercial appliance/SaaS content-security product; the module is not distributed as a standalone downloadable artifact (no public source tarball, distro package, or Maven jar). The security policy PDF describes the module but does not publish a downloadable binary or a checksum. No public artifact could be located.

Could not find any evidence that "Covidence A/S" or a "Covidence FIPS Provider for OpenSSL 3" exists as a real vendor/module. Web searches for the vendor name and module name plus cert 4981 returned no matches on NIST CSRC or elsewhere; results only surfaced unrelated OpenSSL FIPS provider certs (e.g. 4282, 4985) from the actual OpenSSL Project. The provided security_policy_url (140sp4981.pdf) was not independently verified to load/confirm this vendor name. This module record appears fabricated or mislabeled and no public artifact could be located.

Ideem ZSM Cryptographic Module (cert #4982) is a closed vendor MPC/TPC-based mobile crypto module distributed as native shared libraries (libcrypto.so/libsecurikey.so/libssl.so and .dylib variants) embedded within Ideem's proprietary FIDO2/passkey mobile and browser SDKs. Access requires a support-issued Application ID / vendor agreement (per useideem.com); no public download of the compiled module binaries was found. The public npm package @ideem/zsm-client-sdk (registry.npmjs.org) is a pure JS/TS client wrapper for the browser API and does not bundle the validated native module or any matching checksums for it. No repomd/Maven/GitHub release artifact matching the validated filenames was found on Ideem's GitHub (github.com/useideem, 2 repos, unrelated). No public SHA-256 for the validated binaries exists. Returning found=false per instructions rather than padding with speculative artifacts.

published_artifacts.evidence: OpenSSL 3.1.2 is publicly documented (openssl-library.org blog post 'OpenSSL 3.1.2: FIPS 140-3 Validated', and CMVP cert #4985 listing) as the exact source version submitted for and receiving FIPS 140-3 validation cert #4985, valid until 2030-03-10, and stated to be compatible/reusable by OpenSSL 3.0-3.5 builds via the same FIPS provider source module. The sha256 for the openssl-3.1.2.tar.gz source tarball was read from conan-center-index's conandata.yml, which annotates this exact version as 'Most recent FIPS validated as of 2025-03-14' — corroborating it is the validated source. This is the upstream source tarball from which fips.so/fips.dll/fips.dylib (the validated module filenames per the security policy) are built; it is not a hash published directly by OpenSSL/CMVP, hence confidence <1.0.

provenance.trackB_notes: Module: OpenSSL FIPS Provider, cert #4985, validated software version = OpenSSL 3.1.2 (per openssl-library.org's official 2025-03-11 post 'OpenSSL 3.1.2: FIPS 140-3 Validated' and the CMVP certificate detail page https://csrc.nist.gov/projects/cryptographic-module-validation-program/certificate/4985). The security policy PDF at the given URL was fetched but returned as opaque binary/compressed stream text via WebFetch, so I could not directly read version/hash text from it; version was corroborated instead via OpenSSL's own announcement and third-party build-recipe metadata (conan-center-index) that explicitly flags 3.1.2 as the FIPS-validated source as of the relevant date. The validated artifact itself is a source module (fips.so/.dll/.dylib per the module's listed filenames) built from the openssl-3.1.2.tar.gz source tree via the documented FIPS provider build+install process (`./Configure enable-fips`, `make`, `make install_fips`) — there is no single canonical pre-built binary published by the OpenSSL Project for fips.so/dll/dylib; distros and vendors each build their own from this same validated source. No SHA-256 was found published directly on openssl.org's own download/checksum pages during this session (search results returned only third-party mirrors), so the hash is sourced from conan-center-index's recipe file rather than an official OpenSSL checksum page — treat as good corroboration, not a first-party checksum.

provenance.trackB_notes: Samsung BoringCrypto Android (cert 5012) is a vendor-embedded build of BoringSSL's crypto module compiled into specific Samsung device firmware (Galaxy S20/S21/S22/S23, Tab Active3/S9 FE, XCover series, Z Flip 4, etc.) running Android 13, tested with/without ARMv8 PAA across Snapdragon and Exynos SoCs. Samsung does not publish this crypto module as a standalone downloadable artifact (no jar/deb/rpm/tarball with a version tag corresponding to the cert); it is only obtainable by acquiring a full Samsung firmware image (e.g. via Samsung Firmware Update Server / Odin-compatible ROMs) and extracting the relevant native library, and even then there is no public way to confirm which build maps 1:1 to software version "2022061300" without vendor cooperation. The security policy PDF (140sp5012.pdf) itself is public but does not embed a hash of the binary. No published checksum exists that I could locate. Searches were bounded to confirm this is a closed vendor-embedded module with no public standalone release, consistent with other Samsung/Motorola/Google BoringCrypto Android certs.

published_artifacts.evidence: Provides libfreeblpriv3.so, one of the exact filenames listed on the cert. The AL2023 NSS security policy (140sp5014.pdf, Table 2) lists the tested module version as 'libfreeblpriv3.so 3.90.0-3e9a8358c972db98' - i.e. upstream NSS 3.90.0 tagged with an internal git-commit build identifier, not an rpm release number. The AL2023 core repodata currently offers nss-softokn-freebl 3.90.0 across rpm releases 2,3,6,7.amzn2023.x; release 7.amzn2023.0.1 is the newest 3.90.0 build before the package moved to 3.112.0, making it the most plausible match to the commit-hash-tagged validated build, but this was not confirmed against the exact git commit 3e9a8358c972db98.
Provides libsoftokn3.so (and libfsoftokn3.so FIPS indicator library), the other filenames listed on the cert. Same version-matching caveat as the freebl package.

provenance.trackB_notes: Cert 5014's security policy (140sp5014.pdf) confirms: Module Type=Software, files libsoftokn3.so and libfreeblpriv3.so, tested/vendor-affirmed version string '3.90.0-3e9a8358c972db98' (upstream NSS 3.90.0 + AWS internal git build hash), tested on AL2023 EC2 c7g.metal (Graviton3) and c6i.metal (Xeon Platinum 8375C), with/without PAA, integrity check via DSA signature (2048-bit key, SHA-256). AWS does not publish a standalone tarball of this exact commit-pinned build; the shippable deliverable is the Amazon Linux 2023 RPM packages nss-softokn (ships libsoftokn3.so, libfsoftokn3.so) and nss-softokn-freebl (ships libfreeblpriv3.so), built from source rpm nss-3.90.0-*.amzn2023.src.rpm via koji.amazonlinux. I confirmed via AL2023's public repodata (cdn.amazonlinux.com, core repo, live as of 2026-07) that multiple 3.90.0 rpm releases (2,3,6,7.amzn2023.x) exist with sha256 checksums readable directly from primary.xml.gz. I picked the newest 3.90.0 release (7.amzn2023.0.1) as the closest plausible candidate to the validated commit-pinned build, but could NOT cryptographically confirm it is the exact commit 3e9a8358c972db98 used for FIPS testing (would require pulling and diffing the nss-3.90.0-7.amzn2023.src.rpm sources against that commit, which was out of scope for the time budget) - treat sha256 values as belonging to *a* nss-softokn/nss-softokn-freebl 3.90.0 AL2023 build, not verified as *the* validated one. AWS's FIPS compliance page and AL2023 FIPS 140-3 announcement blog confirm NSS is part of the validated module set but do not themselves publish artifact hashes.

published_artifacts.evidence: Security policy (140sp5015.pdf) explicitly states: 'sudo yum install gnutls-3.8.0-376.amzn2023.0.2' and identifies module version 3.8.0-f3d7c0863662248d as the validated module. Package is distributed via AL2023 official yum/dnf repos hosted at cdn.amazonlinux.com (region-specific S3-backed CDN, not a single stable public URL); could not retrieve a readable repomd/checksum listing within search budget (amazonlinux.pkgs.org and cdn.amazonlinux.com direct fetch both blocked/paywalled for this session).

The security policy PDF (140sp5017.pdf) itself was not fetched to check for embedded hashes, but such policies rarely publish binary SHA-256 values. Concluding this is a closed vendor appliance module with no fishable public artifact, consistent with guidance to return found=false rather than pad with a speculative match.

Trellix FIPS Provider (Musarubra US LLC), cert 5019, is an OpenSSL 3.x-based FIPS provider (fips.so) embedded within Trellix's commercial security products (e.g., Endpoint Security, DLP, IPS, Drive Encryption). Trellix's FIPS-mode documentation describes enabling FIPS mode within their installed products rather than distributing the provider module independently. This is consistent with a closed vendor-embedded module (similar to many enterprise security-appliance FIPS providers) rather than an openly downloadable/buildable artifact like Bouncy Castle or an RPM package. No hash fabricated. Returning found=false per instructions since no public downloadable artifact could be identified.

SSH Communications Security Cryptographic Module (cert #5020) is a proprietary shared library (fips.so) embedded in SSH Communications Security's commercial products (PrivX PAM, Tectia SSH Client/Server). No standalone public download of fips.so or the module source/binary was found — it ships only bundled inside licensed commercial product installers, which are not freely downloadable and require a customer license. No public checksum, package repository, or Maven/NPM-style artifact exists for this module. Searched CMVP certificate page and vendor product pages; found only marketing/datasheet pages, no direct artifact links.

published_artifacts.evidence: AWS docs (docs.aws.amazon.com/linux/al2023/ug/fips-openssl-swap-provider.html) state that on AL2023.7+, the openssl-fips-provider-certified package 'will match the checksum on the NIST certificate' matching security policy version string 'Amazon Linux 2023 - OpenSSL FIPS Provider version: 3.0.8-d694bfa693b76001'. The -so subpackage (openssl-fips-provider-certified-so) is the one that actually ships /usr/lib64/ossl-modules/fips.so — confirmed via 'rpm:conflicts openssl-libs' and file size ~596KB matching a shared object. sha256 read directly from AL2023 core repo primary.xml.gz metadata retrieved from the official Amazon Linux CDN mirror.

Metapackage/wrapper for openssl-fips-provider-certified-so; requires the -so package (which actually contains fips.so) at the exact matching version. Included for completeness since users typically 'dnf install' this name; sha256 read from same primary.xml.gz metadata.

provenance.trackB_notes: Module: NIST cert 5021, Amazon Linux 2023 OpenSSL FIPS Provider, boundary = fips.so shared library. AWS's own docs confirm the 'openssl-fips-provider-certified' package (pinned version) 'will match the checksum on the NIST certificate,' distinct from 'openssl-fips-provider-latest' which drifts with updates. Version string from AL2023 docs matches security policy naming: 'Amazon Linux 2023 - OpenSSL FIPS Provider version: 3.0.8-d694bfa693b76001', RPM package version 3.0.8-1.amzn2023.0.1. Located the exact RPM and its sha256 by pulling the live AL2023 'core' yum repo (cdn.amazonlinux.com/al2023/core) repomd.xml -> primary.xml.gz and grepping the package entries directly — this is primary distro repository metadata, not a third-party mirror. Two related packages exist: openssl-fips-provider-certified (thin wrapper) and openssl-fips-provider-certified-so (contains the actual shared object /usr/lib64/ossl-modules/fips.so); reported both, with the -so package as the primary/higher-confidence artifact since it's the one whose contents form the cryptographic boundary. Could not independently verify against a published NIST/AWS-stated hash for fips.so itself (the security policy PDF likely states an internal HMAC/self-test digest rather than an RPM sha256), so confidence capped below 1.0. Did not attempt to fetch/parse the 140sp5021.pdf itself for an internal module digest — that would be a different kind of hash (integrity check value) than a package sha256 and was out of scope given time bounds.

published_artifacts.evidence: Security policy 140sp5022.pdf (read directly) states the module is version '3.90.0-4408e3bb8a34af3a' of the Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 NSS Cryptographic Module; cryptographic boundary = Softoken (libsoftokn3.so) and Freebl (libfreeblpriv3.so) libraries, integrity-checked via HMAC-SHA-256 against .chk files (Table 2/3, p.10-11). Tested on RHEL 9 on Dell PowerEdge R440, IBM 9080-HEX/POWER10, IBM z16 3931-A01. These libraries ship in RPM packages nss-softokn / nss-softokn-freebl. A public build for the matching upstream 3.90.0 version exists for el9 (nss-softokn-freebl-3.90.0-7.el9, released 2024-04-10, per rpmfind.net / CentOS Stream 9 AppStream listings found via search), but repeated attempts to fetch the actual rpmfind.net page (404s on both www and fr2 mirrors) and pkgs.org (402 paywall) prevented reading a real SHA256. Red Hat's actual RHEL 9 nss-softokn RPM ships via the entitled Red Hat CDN, not a fully public anonymous URL; CentOS Stream 9 is the closest public rebuild of the same upstream source, but the exact NVR/patch set matching the validated '-4408e3bb8a34af3a' build tag could not be confirmed byte-for-byte.

provenance.trackB_notes: Confirmed via the security policy PDF (read directly, not just search snippets) that the validated module is version 3.90.0-4408e3bb8a34af3a, cryptographic boundary limited to libsoftokn3.so (Softoken) + libfreeblpriv3.so (Freebl), tested on RHEL 9 across x86_64 (Dell PowerEdge), ppc64le (IBM POWER10/PowerVM), and s390x (IBM z16). These libraries are shipped inside the nss-softokn / nss-softokn-freebl RPM packages. Public CentOS Stream 9 rebuilds of nss-softokn 3.90.0 exist (e.g. nss-softokn-freebl-3.90.0-7.el9) and are likely close analogs, but I could not reach a live rpmfind.net or pkgs.org page to actually read a SHA256, so sha256 is null per the hard rule against fabrication. The true RHEL 9 (entitled) RPM is behind Red Hat's subscriber CDN and not freely/anonymously downloadable, which is common for RHEL distro modules — CentOS Stream is offered as the closest public proxy artifact, not a confirmed byte-identical match.

Cert #5023 "OpenSSL Cryptographic Module" (F5, Inc.) is a software library (libcrypto.so.1.0.2) bundled specifically for the F5 VELOS system controller/blade platform, per the CMVP entry: "cryptographic library offering various cryptographic mechanisms to be used by OpenSSL application running on F5 VELOS system controller and blade." This is proprietary appliance firmware, not a redistributable upstream OpenSSL build or a package published on any public repository (no RPM/DEB, no Maven, no GitHub release). F5 does not publish standalone downloads of VELOS OS internal libraries; access requires an F5 support contract/entitlement. No public source tarball, distro package, or checksum could be located for this exact validated artifact. Searches performed: NIST CMVP certificate page (confirmed vendor/module/platform binding) and general web search for the cert number/filename (no hits beyond CMVP/NIST security-policy listing). Given the closed, platform-bound nature of this module, returning found=false with no artifacts rather than padding with a speculative match.

Validated module: NSS cryptography module for AlmaLinux 9, version 3.90.0-b84457b0165f79bf (libsoftokn3.so, libfreeblpriv3.so + .chk files), per the security policy (https://csrc.nist.gov/CSRC/media/projects/cryptographic-module-validation-program/documents/security-policies/140sp5031.pdf). The security policy identifies the exact shipping RPMs as nss-softokn-3.90.0-6.el9_2.tuxcare.1, nss-softokn-freebl-3.90.0-6.el9_2.tuxcare.1, and nss-tools-3.90.0-6.el9_2.tuxcare.1, built for AlmaLinux 9.2 and signed with TuxCare's RSA-4096 build key.

These specific .tuxcare.1 RPMs are NOT publicly downloadable: they are distributed only through TuxCare's paid AlmaLinux Extended Security Updates (ESU) repository, gated by an ESU license key (confirmed via docs.tuxcare.com/errata.tuxcare.com — the errata advisory URL guessed returned 404, and TuxCare's own docs state ESU package access requires an active subscription). Plain upstream AlmaLinux 9 nss-softokn packages (e.g. nss-softokn-3.90.0-6.el9_3 from the standard BaseOS repo) exist publicly but are NOT the validated TuxCare-patched build (different release tag, not FIPS-certified per this cert), so I did not report them as a match — reporting them would misrepresent an unvalidated artifact as the certified one. No sha256 was found published anywhere for the exact .tuxcare.1 build. Returning found=false per instructions since no publicly downloadable artifact corresponding to the validated build exists.

published_artifacts.evidence: Security policy (140sp5032.pdf, section 11.4 'Component versioning identification') states the module's 'Show version information' service reports 'Version: QAT Engine v1.3.1', confirming the validated qatprovider.so is built from Intel QAT_Engine tag v1.3.1 (github.com/intel/QAT_Engine). The GitHub release v1.3.1 (published 2023-08-23, commit a5a0bfe) has no binary release assets attached (verified via GitHub API - assets: []), only auto-generated tarball_url/zipball_url source archives; no published checksum found on the release page or API response.

Security policy section 11.3 (Installation) gives the exact install command: 'rpm -ivh qatprovider-fips-1.3.1-1.el9.x86_64.rpm --target noarch', built for RHEL 9.0 with OpenSSL 3.0.8 (matches the tested configuration). Policy states 'the RPM package can be downloaded from Intel GitHub repository' but the QAT_Engine GitHub releases page for v1.3.1 has no attached binary assets (confirmed via GitHub API), so no concrete public download URL or hash could be located; the RPM appears to be built by the operator from source per docs/install.md using the fips/qatengine_fips.spec file (github.com/intel/QAT_Engine/blob/master/qatengine_fips.spec).
Security policy component-versioning output (p.31) lists 'IPSec-mb version: v1.3', matching Intel's intel-ipsec-mb GitHub repository release v1.3. No checksum read from a page.

Security policy component-versioning output (p.31) lists 'IPP-crypto version: ippcp_2021.7.1', matching a tag/release in Intel's ipp-crypto GitHub repository (asymmetric-key crypto library providing libcrypto_mb.so). No checksum read from a page.

provenance.trackB_notes: Read the full non-proprietary security policy (140sp5032.pdf). Module is Intel QAT Provider v1.3.1, a software-hybrid OpenSSL 3.0.8 provider (qatprovider.so) tested on RHEL 9.0 with an Eagle Stream/StreamEagle Xeon Platinum 8488C server. The policy pins exact component versions via its 'Show version information' service output: QAT Engine v1.3.1, QAT_HW Driver QAT20.l.1.0.40-00004, IPSec-mb v1.3, IPP-crypto ippcp_2021.7.1, and hardware device 'Intel Corporation Device 4940 (rev 40)'. It also gives the literal install command 'rpm -ivh qatprovider-fips-1.3.1-1.el9.x86_64.rpm --target noarch' and states the RPM is obtainable from the Intel GitHub repository (github.com/intel/QAT_Engine). However, the actual GitHub release for tag v1.3.1 (verified via GitHub API) carries zero binary assets -- only Github auto-generated source tarball/zipball links -- so the FIPS RPM itself does not appear to be publicly published as a downloadable binary; users appear to build it themselves from source using fips/qatengine_fips.spec (or equivalent) per docs/install.md. No SHA-256 (or any) checksum for any of these artifacts was found published on any page I could read. This is consistent with Intel's QAT_Engine project generally not publishing precompiled release binaries or checksums for its GitHub releases (only source code tags).
