published_artifacts.filename: gnutls-3.7.6-39e2433a29b33b55 (libgnutls.so.30, libnettle.so.8, libhogweed.so.6, libgmp.so.10, statically linked)
published_artifacts.evidence: FIPS 140-3 Security Policy (csrc.nist.gov/CSRC/media/projects/cryptographic-module-validation-program/documents/security-policies/140sp5037.pdf) states: 'This document is the non-proprietary FIPS 140-3 Security Policy for version 3.7.6-39e2433a29b33b55 of the Oracle 9 GnuTLS Cryptographic Module.' Table 2 confirms Package/File Name = libgnutls.so.30; libnettle.so.8; libhogweed.so.6; libgmp.so.10 (statically linked to libgnutls); libgnutls.so.30.hmac, Software/Firmware Version 3.7.6-39e2433a29b33b55, Integrity Test HMAC-SHA-256. Oracle docs (docs.oracle.com/en/operating-systems/oracle-linux/9/security/fips_validated_modules.html) identify the shipping RPM as gnutls with an '_fips' suffixed version (epoch 10) distributed via the ol9_u3_security_validation ULN channel / yum repo (ol9_x86_64_u3_security_validation, ol9_aarch64_u3_security_validation). This repo requires an Oracle Linux support/ULN subscription to browse or download, so no public checksum was located; the RPM itself is not freely downloadable without such access.

provenance.trackB_notes: Confirmed exact validated software version string (3.7.6-39e2433a29b33b55) directly from the CMVP-published Security Policy PDF (140sp5037.pdf, Oracle Linux 9 GnuTLS Cryptographic Module, Document Version 1.4, dated Aug 28 2025). Module = libgnutls.so.30 + libnettle.so.8 + libhogweed.so.6 + libgmp.so.10 (gmp/nettle/hogweed statically linked into libgnutls), integrity-verified via HMAC-SHA-256 against libgnutls.so.30.hmac. Shipping vehicle is the Oracle Linux 9 'gnutls' RPM with an '_fips' version suffix (epoch 10), distributed only through Oracle's ULN 'ol9_u3_security_validation' channel / matching yum repo per Oracle's own FIPS-validated-modules docs (docs.oracle.com/en/operating-systems/oracle-linux/9/security/fips_validated_modules.html) — this repo requires an active Oracle Linux support subscription to access, so I could not reach a package index or repodata to read a real sha256. No sha256 was fabricated; sha256 fields are null. Tested on 3 hardware platforms (Xeon Platinum 8358, AMD EPYC 7J13, Ampere Altra Q80-30) under KVM on Oracle Linux 8, all with the identical software version string, so there is effectively one artifact identity across configurations.

published_artifacts.evidence: Security policy (140sp5158.pdf) states Module version 3.1.2, filenames fips.so/fips.dll/fips.dylib, built from the OpenSSL FIPS Provider, and explicitly lists 'OpenSSL 3.1.2 FIPS 140-3 Validation Contributors: KeyPair Consulting Inc' -- identical attribution language to OpenSSL's own upstream FIPS provider cert (#4985), indicating Dell built this module from the standard OpenSSL 3.1.2 source release rather than a Dell-modified fork. This is the upstream source tarball, not a Dell-published binary -- confidence reduced because Dell's actual compiled fips.so/dll/dylib binaries and their build flags/toolchain are not publicly published or independently verifiable as bit-identical to a self-build.

provenance.trackB_notes: Cert 5158 is Dell's own FIPS 140-3 validation of a build of the OpenSSL FIPS Provider (module version 3.1.2), tested across Ubuntu 22.04.1, Debian 11.5, FreeBSD 13.1, Windows 10 Pro, and macOS 11.5.2 on various Dell/Apple hardware (Lightship Security lab, validated 2/13/2026). Dell does not publish standalone downloadable fips.so/fips.dll/fips.dylib binaries or their hashes -- these ship embedded within Dell products/tooling, not as a standalone public download. The one verifiable public artifact is the upstream OpenSSL 3.1.2 source tarball (sha256 read directly from openssl.org's published .sha256 file), which is the most likely build basis given the identical FIPS validation contributor attribution to OpenSSL's own provider policy. No Dell-specific compiled artifact or checksum could be located publicly.

rows.module_name: BigFix Encryption Module based on the OpenSSL FIPS Provider

published_artifacts.evidence: Security Policy (140sp5159.pdf) Section 11.1 Installation, Initialization, and Startup Procedures states verbatim: 'The Module is installed as part of the OpenSSL 3.1.2 library. The source distribution package is located at https://www.openssl.org/source/openssl-3.1.2.tar.gz.' Build procedure: ./Configure enable-fips; make; make install (or 'make install_fips' to build just the FIPS provider, producing fips.so/fips.dll/fips.dylib, HMAC-SHA2-256 integrity tested, module version 3.1.2 per Table 2). This is the exact upstream source the vendor built the validated module from; the SHA-256 was read directly from OpenSSL's own published checksum file for this release (confirmed 64 hex chars via curl).

These are the actual validated binary artifact names per Table 2 of the security policy (HMAC-SHA2-256 integrity-tested). They are build outputs of the OpenSSL 3.1.2 FIPS provider (via 'make install_fips') and are not separately published/downloadable by HCL with a vendor-issued hash — HCL only distributes the module bundled inside BigFix product installers, and the security policy itself directs integrators to build from the public OpenSSL 3.1.2 source tarball rather than download a precompiled binary.

provenance.trackB_notes: Module is a vendor (HCL/Lightship Security) validation of the stock OpenSSL 3.1.2 FIPS Provider, not a distinct HCL-authored crypto library. Security policy 140sp5159.pdf explicitly names the exact upstream source: https://www.openssl.org/source/openssl-3.1.2.tar.gz, built via './Configure enable-fips && make && make install' (or 'make install_fips') to produce the validated fips.so/fips.dll/fips.dylib (Table 2, HMAC-SHA2-256 integrity). I fetched OpenSSL's own published .sha256 checksum file for openssl-3.1.2.tar.gz and confirmed it directly (64-char hex, read via curl, not computed/guessed). No separate HCL-published binary or hash exists for fips.so/dll/dylib — those are build artifacts assembled during BigFix's own build process, not offered as a standalone public download with a checksum.
